pages.title: Introduction
pages.html:
Introduction

In the first part of this introduction, we give some details on the content of the present volume; in the second, on the whole of the “ Séminaire de Géométrie Algébrique du Bois-Marie ”, of which the present volume constitutes the first tome.

1

The present volume sets forth the foundations of a theory of the fundamental group in Algebraic Geometry, from the “kroneckerian” point of view which permits treating on the same footing the case of an algebraic variety in the usual sense, and that of a ring of integers of a number field, for example. This point of view can be expressed in a satisfactory way only in the language of schemes, and we shall freely use this language, as well as the principal results set forth in the first three chapters of the Éléments de Géométrie Algébrique of J. Dieudonné and A. Grothendieck, (cited EGA in what follows). The study of the present volume of the “ Séminaire de Géométrie Algébrique du Bois-Marie ” does not require other knowledge of Algebraic Geometry, and can therefore serve as an introduction to the current techniques of Algebraic Geometry, for a reader wishing to familiarize himself with these techniques.

Exposés I to XI of this book are a textual reproduction, practically unchanged, of the mimeographed notes of the oral Seminar, which were distributed by the care of the Institut des Hautes Études Scientifiques1. We have limited ourselves to adding a few footnotes to the original text, correcting a few typographical errors, and making a terminological adjustment, the word “ simple morphism ” having notably been replaced in the meantime by that of “ smooth morphism ”, which does not lend itself to the same confusions.

Exposés I to IV present the local notions of étale morphism and smooth morphism; they hardly use the language of schemes, set forth in Chapter I of the Éléments2. Exposé V presents the axiomatic description of the fundamental group of a scheme, useful even in the classical case where this scheme reduces to the spectrum of a field, where one finds a most convenient reformulation of the usual Galois theory. Exposés VI and VIII present descent theory, which has taken on a growing importance in Algebraic Geometry in these last years, and which could render analogous services in Analytic Geometry and in Topology. It is fitting to point out that Exposé VII had not been written, and its substance is incorporated in a work of J. Giraud (Méthode de la Descente, Bull. Soc. Math. France, Mémoire 2, 1964, viii + 150 p.). In Exposé IX, one studies more specifically the descent of étale morphisms, obtaining a systematic approach for theorems of the Van Kampen type for the fundamental group, which appear here as mere translations of descent theorems. It is essentially a matter of a procedure for computing the fundamental group of a connected scheme \(X\), equipped with a surjective and proper morphism, say \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), in terms of the fundamental groups of the connected components of \(X'\) and of the fibre products \(X'\times_X X'\), \(X'\times_X X'\times_X X'\), and of the homomorphisms induced between these groups by the canonical simplicial morphisms between the preceding schemes. Exposé X gives the theory of the specialization of the fundamental group, for a proper and smooth morphism, whose most striking result consists in the determination (up to little) of the fundamental group of a smooth algebraic curve in characteristic \(p>0\), thanks to the result known by transcendental means in characteristic zero. Exposé XI gives some examples and complements, notably making explicit in cohomological form the theory of the coverings of Kummer, and that of Artin-Schreier. For other comments on the text, see the Avertissement to the multigraphed version, which follows the present Introduction.

Since the writing in 1961 of the present Seminar there has been developed, in collaboration by M. Artin and myself, the language of the étale topology and a corresponding cohomological theory, set forth in the part SGA 4 “ Cohomologie étale des schémas ” of the Séminaire de Géométrie Algébrique, to appear in the same series as the present volume. This language, and the results at its disposal already at present, furnish a particularly supple tool for the study of the fundamental group, permitting a better understanding of and a going beyond certain of the results set forth here. There would therefore be reason to take up entirely again the theory of the fundamental group from this point of view (all the key results in fact figuring already at present in loc. cit. ). This is what was projected for the chapter of the Éléments devoted to the fundamental group, which was to contain likewise several other developments which could not find their place here (relying on the technique of resolution of singularities): computation of the “ local fundamental group ” of a complete local ring in terms of a suitable resolution of singularities of this ring, local and global Künneth formulas for the fundamental group without hypothesis of properness (cf. Exp. XIII), the results of M. Artin on the comparison of the local fundamental groups of an excellent Henselian local ring and of its completion (SGA 4 XIX). Let us likewise point out the necessity of developing a theory of the fundamental group of a topos, which will encompass at once the ordinary topological theory, its semi-simplicial version, the “ profinite ” variant developed in Exposé V of the present volume, and the somewhat more general pro-discrete variant of SGA 3 X 7 (adapted to the case of non-normal and non-unibranch schemes). While awaiting an overall recasting of the theory in this perspective, Exposé XIII of Mme Raynaud, using the language and the results of SGA 4, is destined to show the advantage one can draw in a few typical questions, notably generalizing certain results of Exposé X to non-proper relative schemes. There one gives in particular the structure of the “ prime to \(p\) ” fundamental group of a non-complete algebraic curve in arbitrary characteristic (which I had announced in 1959, but of which a proof had not been published to this day).

Despite these numerous lacunae and imperfections (others will say: because of these lacunae and imperfections), I think that the present volume may be useful to the reader who wishes to familiarize himself with the theory of the fundamental group, as well as as a work of reference, while awaiting the writing and the appearance of a text escaping the criticisms which I have just enumerated.

2

The present volume constitutes tome 1 of the “ Séminaire de Géométrie Algébrique du Bois-Marie ”, whose following volumes are planned to appear in the same series as this one. The aim that the Séminaire proposes for itself, parallel to the treatise “ Éléments de Géométrie Algébrique ” of J. Dieudonné and A. Grothendieck, is to lay the foundations of Algebraic Geometry, following the points of view in this last work. The standard reference for all the volumes of the Séminaire is constituted by Chapters I, II, III of the “ Éléments de Géométrie Algébrique ” (cited EGA I, II, III), and one supposes the reader in possession of the baggage of commutative algebra and homological algebra which these chapters entail3. Moreover, in each volume of the Séminaire reference will be made freely, to the measure of the needs, to earlier volumes of the same Séminaire, or to other chapters published or on the point of appearing of the “ Éléments ”.

Each part of the Séminaire is centered on a principal subject, indicated in the title of the corresponding volume or volumes; the oral seminar generally bears on one academic year, sometimes more. The exposés inside each part of the Séminaire are generally in a close logical dependence upon one another; on the other hand, the different parts of the Séminaire are to a large extent logically independent of one another. Thus, the part “ Schémas en Groupes ” is almost entirely independent of the two parts of the Séminaire which chronologically precede it; on the other hand, it makes frequent appeal to the results of EGA IV. Here is the list of the parts of the Séminaire which are to appear soon (cited SGA 1 to SGA 7 in what follows):

  1. SGA 1.

    Étale coverings and fundamental group, 1960 and 1961.

  2. SGA 2.

    Local cohomology of coherent sheaves and local and global Lefschetz theorems, 1961/62.

  3. SGA 3.

    Group schemes, 1963 and 1964 (3 volumes), in collab. with M. Demazure.

  4. SGA 4.

    Theory of topoi and étale cohomology of schemes, 1963/64 (3 volumes), (in collab. with M. Artin and J.L. Verdier).

  5. SGA 5.

    \(l\)-adic cohomology and \(L\)-functions, 1964 and 1965 (2 volumes).

  6. SGA 6.

    Theory of intersections and Riemann-Roch theorem, 1966/67 (2 volumes) (in collab. with P. Berthelot and L. Illusie).

  7. SGA 7.

    Groups of local monodromy in algebraic geometry.

Three among these partial Seminars were directed in collaboration with other mathematicians, who will appear as co-authors on the cover of the corresponding volumes. As for the other active participants of the Seminar, whose role (both from the editorial point of view and from that of the work of mathematical refinement) grew from year to year, the name of each participant appears at the head of the exposés for which he is responsible as lecturer or as editor, and the list of those who appear in a given volume is indicated on the flyleaf of the said volume.

It is appropriate to give some details on the relations between the Seminar and the Éléments. The latter were intended in principle to give an overall exposition of the notions and techniques deemed most fundamental in Algebraic Geometry, as these notions and techniques themselves emerge, through the natural interplay of requirements of logical and aesthetic coherence. From this standpoint, it was natural to regard the Seminar as a preliminary version of the Éléments, intended to be incorporated almost entirely, sooner or later, into the latter. This process had already begun to a certain extent some years ago, since exposés I to IV of the present volume SGA 1 are entirely incorporated into EGA IV, and exposés VI to VIII were to be so within a few years in EGA VI. However, as the work of construction undertaken in the Éléments and in the Seminar develops, and as the overall proportions become clearer, the initial principle (according to which the Seminar would constitute only a preliminary and provisional version) appears less and less realistic on account (among other things) of the limits imposed by provident nature on the span of human life. Given the care generally taken in the writing of the various parts of the Seminar, there will no doubt be occasion to take up such a part again in the Éléments (or in treatises that would take over from them) only when advances subsequent to the writing make it possible to bring very substantial improvements to it, at the price of rather profound modifications. This is already the case at present for the present seminar SGA 1, as was said above, and also for SGA 2 (thanks to the recent results of Mme Raynaud). On the other hand, nothing indicates at present that this will be so in the near future for any of the parts cited above SGA 3 to SGA 7.

References within the “Séminaire de Géométrie Algébrique du Bois Marie” are given as follows. An internal reference to one of the parts SGA 1 to SGA 7 of the Seminar is given in the style III 9.7, where the numeral III designates the number of the exposé, which appears at the top of each page of the exposé in question, and 9.7 the number of the statement (or of the definition, remark, etc.) within the exposé. Where appropriate, longer decimal numbers may be used, for example 9.7.1, 9.7.2 to designate, for example, the various steps in the proof of a proposition 9.7. The reference III 9 designates paragraph 9 of exposé III. The number of the exposé is omitted for references internal to an exposé. For a reference to another of the parts of the Seminar, one uses the same symbols, but preceded by the mention of the part in question of the SGA, SGA 1 III 9.7. Likewise, the reference EGA IV 11.5.7 means: Éléments de Géométrie Algébrique, Chap. IV, statement (or definition etc.) 11.5.7; here, the first Arabic numeral again designates the number of the paragraph. Apart from these conventions in force throughout the SGA as a whole, the bibliography relating to an exposé will generally be gathered at the end of it, and will be referred to within the exposé by numbers in brackets such as [3], following custom.

Finally, for the reader's convenience, whenever this seems necessary, we shall append at the end of the volumes of the SGA an index of notations, and a terminological index containing, where appropriate, an English translation of the French terms used.

I wish to append to this introduction an extra-mathematical comment. In November 1969 I became aware of the fact that the Institut des Hautes Études Scientifiques, of which I have been a professor essentially since its foundation, had been receiving for three years subsidies from the Ministry of the Armed Forces. Already as a beginning researcher I found extremely regrettable the lack of scruple shown by most scientists in agreeing to collaborate in one form or another with the military apparatus. My motivations at that time were essentially of a moral nature, and hence unlikely to be taken seriously. Today they acquire a new force and dimension, in view of the danger of destruction of the human species with which we are threatened by the proliferation of military apparatus and of the means of mass destruction that these apparatus have at their disposal. I have explained myself elsewhere in a more detailed way on these questions, which are much more important than the advancement of any science whatever (including mathematics); one may for example consult on this subject the article by G. Edwards in issue 1 of the journal Survivre (August 1970), summarizing a more detailed exposition of these questions that I had given elsewhere. Thus, I found myself working for three years in an institution while it was participating, without my knowledge, in a mode of financing that I consider immoral and dangerous4. Being at present the only one to hold this opinion among my colleagues at the IHES, which doomed to failure my efforts to obtain the removal of the military subsidies from the budget of the IHES, I have taken the decision that was called for and am leaving the IHES on 30 September 1970 and am also suspending all scientific collaboration with this institution, as long as it continues to accept such subsidies. I have asked M. Motchane, director of the IHES, that the IHES refrain, from 1 October 1970, from distributing mathematical texts of which I am the author, or which form part of the Séminaire de Géométrie Algébrique du Bois Marie. As was said above, the distribution of this seminar will be ensured by the house of Julius Springer, in the series of the Lecture Notes. I am happy to thank here the house of Springer and Mr K. Peters for the effective and courteous help they have given me in making this publication possible, by taking charge in particular of the typing for the photo-offset of the new exposés added to the old seminars, and of the missing exposés of the incomplete seminars.

I also thank M. J.P. Delale, who took charge of the thankless work of compiling the index of notations and the terminological index.

Massy, August 1970.

footnotes.html: As well as the notes of the seminars following this one. This mode of distribution having proved impracticable and insufficient in the long run, all the “ Séminaire de Géométrie Algébrique du Bois-Marie ” will henceforth appear in the form of a book like the present volume.
A more complete study is now available in the Éléments, Chap IV, §§17 and 18.

See the Introduction to EGA I for
details on this subject.

It goes without saying that the opinion which I have just
expressed engages only my own responsibility, and not that
of the publishing house Springer which publishes the present
volume.
